content:
# Genesis Chapter 13 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 7v–8r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_013_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_013_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 8–9 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** And Abram went up from Egypt, he and his wife and all that he had, and Lot with him, into the Negeb.

**2** Now Abram was very rich in livestock, in silver, and in gold.

**3** And he journeyed on from the Negeb as far as Bethel, to the place where his tent had been at the beginning, between Bethel and Ai,

**4** to the place of the altar that he had made there at the first. And Abram called there on the name of the LORD.

**5** And Lot also, who went with Abram, had flocks and herds and tents.

**6** And the land was not able to bear them dwelling together, for their possessions were great, so that they could not dwell together.

**7** And there was strife between the herdsmen of Abram's livestock and the herdsmen of Lot's livestock. Now the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then dwelling in the land.

**8** So Abram said to Lot, "Let there be no strife between you and me, and between your herdsmen and my herdsmen, for we are kinsmen.

**9** Is not the whole land before you? Separate yourself from me. If you take the left hand, then I will go to the right, or if you take the right hand, then I will go to the left."

**10** And Lot lifted up his eyes and saw all the plain of the Jordan, that it was well watered everywhere like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt, in the direction of Zoar. This was before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah.

**11** So Lot chose for himself all the plain of the Jordan, and Lot journeyed east. Thus they separated from each other.

**12** Abram settled in the land of Canaan, while Lot settled among the cities of the plain and moved his tent as far as Sodom.

**13** Now the men of Sodom were exceedingly wicked and sinful against the LORD.

**14** The LORD said to Abram, after Lot had separated from him, "Lift up your eyes and look from the place where you are, northward and southward and eastward and westward,

**15** for all the land that you see I will give to you and to your offspring forever.

**16** And I will make your offspring as the dust of the earth, so that if one can count the dust of the earth, your offspring also can be counted.

**17** Arise, walk through the length and the breadth of the land, for I will give it to you."

**18** So Abram moved his tent and came and settled by the oaks of Mamre, which are at Hebron, and he built there an altar to the LORD.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 13 spans Folios 7v–8r.

   **Folio 7v** (GEN_013_page_001.jpg): This folio opens with the close of the Egypt narrative from Genesis 12 (verses ~12:14–20) and transitions into Genesis 13. The Masoretic three-column layout is clearly visible. The opening of Genesis 13 is legible in the right-hand column with the characteristic waw-consecutive wayyiqtol verb form וַיַּעַל (vayyaal, "and he went up") initiating the journey back from Egypt. The divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) appears in verse 4 (Abram calling on the name of the LORD) and verse 13 (the men of Sodom were sinful against the LORD). The place-names הַנֶּגֶב (the Negeb), בֵּית אֵל (Bethel), and הָעַי (Ai) are legible in the early verses. The key phrase "the Canaanite and the Perizzite" (הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי) in verse 7 mirrors the narrative aside of Genesis 12:6. The strife narrative and Abram's magnanimous offer to Lot (verses 8–9) are readable in the central column. The critical description of the plain of the Jordan (verse 10) with its double simile is visible. The folio continues through approximately verse 12.

   **Folio 8r** (GEN_013_page_002.jpg): This folio carries the conclusion of Genesis 13 from approximately verse 13 through verse 18, then continues into the opening of Genesis 14. The characterization of Sodom's wickedness in verse 13 is legible: רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים (raim vechattatim, "wicked and sinful"). The LORD's renewed promise to Abram in verses 14–17 is visible with its four-directional command to look — northward, southward, eastward, westward — and the land and seed promises. The "dust of the earth" (עֲפַר הָאָרֶץ) simile in verse 16 is legible. The chapter closes with verse 18: Abram's settlement at the oaks of Mamre near Hebron and the building of an altar to the LORD. The Masoretic markers are visible at the end of the pericope.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. The chapter heading "CAP. XIII" is visible at the top center of page 8 (GEN_013_vaticanus_page_001.jpg). The opening of Genesis 13 in the LXX is readable beginning with Ἀνέβη δὲ Αβραμ ἐξ Αἰγύπτου ("And Abram went up from Egypt"), running through the strife narrative and Lot's choice. Page 9 (GEN_013_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the divine promise to Abram (vv. 14–17) and the settlement at Hebron (v. 18). The LXX text of the Vaticanus agrees closely with the MT throughout Genesis 13, with the key variants noted in the table below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. וַיַּעַל אַבְרָם מִמִּצְרַיִם — "And Abram Went Up from Egypt" (v. 1)**

The chapter opens with the Qal wayyiqtol וַיַּעַל (vayyaal) from the root עָלָה (alah, "to go up, to ascend"). The directional verb is precise: one goes up (עָלָה) from Egypt to Canaan because the land of Canaan is at higher elevation, and because the movement out of Egypt is a movement of moral and theological ascent. The verb stands in deliberate contrast with the verb used at the beginning of the Egypt episode in Genesis 12:10 — וַיֵּרֶד אַבְרָם מִצְרַיְמָה (vayyered Abram Mitsraymah, "and Abram went down to Egypt") — where יָרַד (yarad, "to go down") marked the descent into Egypt.

The formal translation renders "went up" — preserving the directional nuance that distinguishes departure from Egypt as an ascent, consistent with how "going up" from Egypt functions throughout the Hebrew Bible as a metaphor for return to the promised land (compare the Exodus language of עָלָה from Egypt in Numbers 32:11, Deuteronomy 20:1, and throughout).

The LXX renders: Ἀνέβη δὲ Αβραμ ἐξ Αἰγύπτου — "And Abram went up from Egypt" — using ἀναβαίνω (anabainō, "to go up, to ascend"), which precisely corresponds to the Hebrew עָלָה. The LXX and MT are in full agreement on this directional verb.

**2. עָשִׁיר מְאֹד בַּמִּקְנֶה בַּכֶּסֶף וּבַזָּהָב — "Very Rich in Livestock, in Silver, and in Gold" (v. 2)**

The wealth catalogue of verse 2 lists three categories: מִקְנֶה (miqneh, "livestock, cattle, property"), כֶּסֶף (kesef, "silver"), and זָהָב (zahav, "gold"). The word מִקְנֶה derives from the root קָנָה (qanah, "to acquire, to purchase, to possess") and refers comprehensively to acquired livestock — the primary form of moveable wealth in the pastoral economy of the patriarchal narratives.

The adjective עָשִׁיר (ashir, "rich, wealthy") with the intensifier מְאֹד (me'od, "exceedingly, greatly") is unambiguous: Abram is very wealthy. The narrator's statement of this wealth at the opening of the chapter is not incidental — it establishes the material precondition for the problem that follows (v. 6: "the land was not able to bear them dwelling together"). The wealth is the source of the conflict.

The formal translation renders the three-fold catalogue "in livestock, in silver, and in gold," preserving the tripartite structure and the specificity of each category. The LXX renders: ἦν δὲ Αβραμ πλούσιος σφόδρα κτήνεσιν καὶ ἀργυρίῳ καὶ χρυσίῳ — "And Abram was very rich in cattle and silver and gold" — in full agreement with the MT.

**3. וַיִּסַּע לְמַסָּעָיו — "And He Journeyed On" (v. 3) — The Itinerary Formula**

The phrase וַיִּסַּע לְמַסָּעָיו (vayyissa lemaassaiv) is a characteristic itinerary formula of the Abram narrative. The root נָסַע (nasa', "to pull up stakes, to journey, to travel") is specifically the verb for breaking camp — pulling up the tent pegs and moving on. The cognate accusative construction — journeyed his journeyings — gives the phrase a formal, almost ritualized quality. The phrase לְמַסָּעָיו ("to/by his stages," from מַסַּע, massa', "a stage of journey, a camp") implies a retracing of the original route: Abram returns from the Negeb northward to Bethel along the same route by which he had descended toward Egypt.

The formal translation renders "and he journeyed on from the Negeb as far as Bethel" — capturing the verb's movement and the destination without adding the "by stages" construction which would be awkward in English. The LXX renders ἐπορεύθη ὅθεν ἦλθεν ("he went from where he had come"), collapsing the itinerary formula into a summary of return.

The echo of Genesis 12:8 is deliberate: Abram returns to "the place where his tent had been at the beginning" (מְקוֹם הָאֹהֶל, meqom ha'ohel). The phrase "at the beginning" (בַּתְּחִלָּה, batekhillah) is chronologically specific — it was before the Egypt detour. Abram resumes his itinerary of faith by returning to the place of his first altar in Canaan.

**4. לְאֵין יְכֹלֶת לָשֶׁבֶת יַחְדָּו — "They Could Not Dwell Together" (v. 6)**

The statement of verse 6 is structured with careful grammatical layering:

וְלֹא נָשָׂא אֹתָם הָאָרֶץ לָשֶׁבֶת יַחְדָּו

"And the land was not able to bear them dwelling together"

The verb נָשָׂא (nasa', here in the negative: "was not able to bear") means literally "to lift, to carry, to bear." The land is described as incapable of bearing the weight of their combined possessions. This is a remarkable ecological and economic statement: the landscape cannot sustain two great households simultaneously. The problem is abundance, not scarcity — paradoxically, the blessing of wealth (v. 2) generates the conflict (v. 7).

The reason given: כִּי הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב ("for their possessions were great"). The word רְכוּשׁ (rekhush, "property, possessions, wealth") is a comprehensive term — moveable property, goods, substance. The phrase rests on the same root of abundance that characterizes the whole chapter.

The formal translation renders "the land was not able to bear them dwelling together" — preserving the personification of the land as an incapable bearer and the precise statement of the reason.

**5. רִיב — "Strife" (v. 7) — The Nature of the Conflict**

The word רִיב (riv, "strife, contention, dispute, lawsuit") in verse 7 describes the conflict between the herdsmen:

וַיְהִי רִיב בֵּין רֹעֵי מִקְנֵה אַבְרָם וּבֵין רֹעֵי מִקְנֵה לוֹט

"And there was strife between the herdsmen of Abram's livestock and the herdsmen of Lot's livestock."

The word רִיב is a loaded term in Hebrew — it is not merely a quarrel but a legal dispute, a formal contention. In prophetic literature, the same word describes the LORD's covenant lawsuit against Israel (the rib pattern). Here it describes an economic dispute over grazing rights — the most common source of conflict in a pastoral economy where land and water resources are shared. The herdsmen of two wealthy households competing for the same pasturage creates an inherently contentious situation.

The narrative aside that follows — "Now the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then dwelling in the land" — is the same type of compositional marker as 12:6 ("now the Canaanite was then in the land"). It amplifies the stakes: Abram and Lot cannot afford internal conflict when they are surrounded by peoples who already occupy the land. The Perizzite (פְּרִזִּי, perizzi) appears here for the first time in the Genesis narrative — a pre-Israelite inhabitants of Canaan, though their precise identity is uncertain. The formal translation renders "the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then dwelling in the land" — preserving the parallel with 12:6 and the temporal marker "then."

**6. אַנְשֵׁי אֲנָחְנוּ — "For We Are Kinsmen" (v. 8)**

Abram's appeal to Lot in verse 8 rests on a single word: אַחִים (akhim, "brothers, kinsmen"):

כִּי אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים אֲנָחְנוּ

"For we are kinsmen [literally: 'for we are men who are brothers']."

The word אָח (akh, "brother") in Hebrew designates a wide range of relationships — biological brothers, half-brothers, clan members, tribal kin, covenant partners, and close companions. Abram and Lot are specifically uncle and nephew (Genesis 12:5: "Lot his brother's son"), not biological brothers. Yet Abram addresses the relationship as brotherhood — the closest available word for the intimate kinship bond that makes their strife a familial catastrophe rather than merely a business dispute.

The English "kinsmen" captures the family dimension without claiming a precision of relationship the Hebrew does not specify. The LXX renders ἄνθρωποι ἀδελφοί ἐσμεν ἡμεῖς ("we are men who are brothers") — in full formal agreement with the MT.

**7. הַכִּכָּר — "The Plain" (vv. 10–12) — The Jordan Kikkār**

The word כִּכָּר (kikkar, "plain, disk, round region") appears four times in this chapter (vv. 10, 11, 12, and implied) to describe the Jordan Valley region that Lot chooses. The root meaning is "a round flat thing, a disk" — applied to a round loaf of bread (כִּכַּר לֶחֶם), to a talent (the round weight), and to a flat circular plain. In the context of Genesis 13, כִּכַּר הַיַּרְדֵּן (kikkar hayarden, "the plain/disk of the Jordan") designates the flat valley floor of the lower Jordan River and the region of the Dead Sea — a broad, flat, well-watered plain in contrast to the hill country of Canaan.

The formal translation renders "the plain of the Jordan" throughout, which is the standard English equivalent for כִּכַּר הַיַּרְדֵּן. The LXX renders τὸν περίχωρον τοῦ Ιορδάνου ("the surrounding region of the Jordan") — using περίχωρος (perichōros, "surrounding country, region around") for כִּכָּר, which loses the specific circular/flat sense of the Hebrew but preserves the geographic reference.

**8. כְּגַן יְהוָה כְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם — "Like the Garden of the LORD, Like the Land of Egypt" (v. 10) — The Double Simile**

Verse 10 contains one of the most striking double similes in the Hebrew Bible:

כֻּלָּהּ מַשְׁקֶה לִפְנֵי שַׁחֵת יְהוָה אֶת סְדֹם וְאֶת עֲמֹרָה
כְּגַן יְהוָה כְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בֹּאֲכָה צֹעַר

"It was well watered everywhere like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt, in the direction of Zoar. This was before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah."

The double simile has two referents: (a) גַּן יְהוָה (gan YHWH, "the garden of the LORD") — the primeval garden of Eden, the paradigm of abundant fertility and divine provision; and (b) אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם (eretz mitsrayim, "the land of Egypt") — the Nile Valley, whose irrigation system made it the most fertile agricultural land in the ancient Near East, independent of rainfall. The two similes are strikingly juxtaposed: the garden of God (the supernatural ideal) and Egypt (the human pragmatic ideal of agricultural abundance) are placed side by side as equally apt comparisons.

The phrase כֻּלָּהּ מַשְׁקֶה (kullah mashqeh, "all of it watered, the whole of it irrigated") uses the participle of שָׁקָה (shaqah, "to water, to irrigate") — it is a land that waters itself or is watered comprehensively. The formal translation renders "well watered everywhere" — capturing the comprehensive irrigation implied by kullah mashqeh.

The proleptic temporal note — "This was before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah" (לִפְנֵי שַׁחֵת יְהוָה אֶת סְדֹם וְאֶת עֲמֹרָה) — is a striking narratorial intrusion. The narrator knows the outcome: the fertility of the plain that entices Lot will be obliterated by divine judgment. The reader who knows the story of Genesis 19 reads verse 10 with the weight of what is coming. The verb שַׁחֵת (shachet, "to destroy, to corrupt, to ruin") is the same root used in the flood narrative (Genesis 6:13, 17: God "destroyed" the earth). The plain of the Jordan that looks like Eden will be turned to wasteland.

The LXX renders: ὅτι πᾶσα ἦν ποτιζομένη, πρὶν καταστρέψαι τὸν θεὸν Σοδομα καὶ Γομορρα, ὡς ὁ παράδεισος τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ ὡς ἡ γῆ Αἰγύπτου — "for it was all being watered, before God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah, like the paradise of God and like the land of Egypt." The LXX uses παράδεισος τοῦ θεοῦ ("the paradise of God") for גַּן יְהוָה — the same word from which the English "paradise" derives (from the Persian/Greek loan word for a royal garden or pleasure park). The MT's גַּן יְהוָה is "the garden of the LORD" — more intimate and directly Edenic; the LXX's "paradise of God" is slightly more elevated. The formal translation follows the MT: "the garden of the LORD."

**9. וַיִּשָּׂא לוֹט אֶת עֵינָיו — "And Lot Lifted Up His Eyes" (v. 10) — Seeing and Choosing**

The act of looking that determines Lot's choice is expressed with the idiomatic phrase נָשָׂא עֵינַיִם (nasa einayim, "to lift up the eyes") — the standard biblical expression for intentional, deliberate looking. The same expression will be used of Abram in verse 14 when God commands him to look in all directions: "lift up your eyes." The contrast is theologically precise:

- Lot lifts up his eyes and sees the plain of Jordan: he looks by his own initiative, motivated by what appears visually attractive and economically advantageous, and chooses accordingly.
- Abram lifts up his eyes at God's command: he looks because the LORD tells him to look, and what he sees is defined not by natural abundance but by divine promise.

Lot's choice is the choice of sight; Abram's reception is the gift of promise. The formal translation preserves the identical idiom in both verses ("lift up your eyes" / "lifted up his eyes"), allowing the reader to perceive the contrast.

**10. חָטָאִים לַיהוָה מְאֹד — "Exceedingly Wicked and Sinful against the LORD" (v. 13)**

Verse 13 is a dense characterization of Sodom:

וְאַנְשֵׁי סְדֹם רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים לַיהוָה מְאֹד

"Now the men of Sodom were exceedingly wicked and sinful against the LORD."

Two adjectives are used: רָעִים (raim, "evil, wicked, bad") and חַטָּאִים (chattatim, "sinners, those who miss the mark, those who sin"). The first is a comprehensive adjective of moral evil; the second is a noun-as-adjective derived from the root חָטָא (chata', "to sin, to miss, to err") — the standard Hebrew word for sin. The phrase "against the LORD" (לַיהוָה, laYHWH) specifies the vertical dimension of their wickedness: it is not merely social evil but specifically transgression against YHWH.

The intensifier מְאֹד (me'od, "very much, exceedingly, greatly") places the gravity of Sodom's sin at the extreme end of the scale. This brief characterization in verse 13 is the narrative preparation for the judgment of Genesis 19 and for the LORD's announcement to Abraham in Genesis 18:20: "the outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah is great and their sin is very grievous."

The placement of this verse is structurally significant: it is the first thing the narrator tells the reader after Lot has settled "as far as Sodom" (v. 12). Lot has chosen a destination that the narrator immediately characterizes as the most sinful place in the narrative world. The contrast with Abram — who will receive the LORD's promise in the very next verse — is stark. The LXX renders: οἱ δὲ ἄνθρωποι οἱ ἐν Σοδομοις πονηροὶ καὶ ἁμαρτωλοὶ ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ σφόδρα — "the men in Sodom were wicked and sinners before God exceedingly" — in full agreement with the MT in substance, though using ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ ("before God") for לַיהוָה ("against/to the LORD").

**11. The Four-Directional Command — "Northward and Southward and Eastward and Westward" (v. 14)**

After Lot's departure, the LORD commands Abram to look in all four directions:

שָׂא נָא עֵינֶיךָ וּרְאֵה מִן הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה שָׁם
צָפֹנָה וָנֶגְבָּה וָקֵדְמָה וָיָמָּה

"Lift up your eyes and look from the place where you are, northward and southward and eastward and westward"

The four directions צָפוֹן (tsafon, "north"), נֶגֶב (negev, "south"), קֶדֶם (qedem, "east/front"), and יָם (yam, "west/sea") constitute the complete horizon — God gives Abram a comprehensive panoramic vision of the promised land. The instruction uses the same imperative שָׂא (nasa') and the same נָא (na', "I pray, please") of entreaty, followed by וּרְאֵה (ure'eh, "and see/look") — a paired command: lift and see.

The fourfold directional command stands in deliberate contrast to Lot's unilateral upward gaze in verse 10. Lot looked on his own initiative and saw what appealed to him; Abram looks at divine command and is given a comprehensive vision of everything. The contrast between the self-directed seeing and the divinely-commanded seeing is a structural feature of the chapter.

The formal translation renders the four directions as "northward and southward and eastward and westward" — using directional adverbs to preserve the comprehensive four-point compass structure.

**12. כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ — "As the Dust of the Earth" (v. 16) — The Innumerability Promise**

The seed promise of verse 16 uses a characteristically concrete Hebrew simile:

וְשַׂמְתִּי אֶת זַרְעֲךָ כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ
אֲשֶׁר אִם יוּכַל אִישׁ לִמְנוֹת אֶת עֲפַר הָאָרֶץ
גַּם זַרְעֲךָ יִמָּנֶה

"And I will make your offspring as the dust of the earth, so that if one can count the dust of the earth, your offspring also can be counted."

The simile of עֲפַר הָאָרֶץ (afar ha'arets, "dust of the earth") uses the same word for dust used in Genesis 2:7 (God forms Adam from עָפָר, dust) and Genesis 3:19 (you are dust, and to dust you shall return). The word עָפָר (afar) is the fine, granular dust of the dry ground — quintessentially uncountable, innumerable. The promise of descendants "as the dust of the earth" is a promise of absolute innumerability: a number beyond human calculation.

The conditional clause — "if one can count the dust of the earth, your offspring also can be counted" — is a rhetorical condition whose impossibility is self-evident. No one can count dust; therefore no one will be able to count Abram's offspring. The logic of the simile is: the uncountable is the standard; if that standard is met, then the promise is measurable — but the standard can never be met.

The later parallel promise in Genesis 22:17 will use stars as the simile: "I will make your offspring as numerous as the stars of heaven and as the sand on the seashore." The dust of the earth (Genesis 13), the stars of heaven (Genesis 15:5), and the sand of the seashore (Genesis 22:17) together constitute a trilogy of innumerability promises, each using a different natural phenomenon that exceeds human counting.

The LXX renders: ὡς τὴν ἄμμον τῆς γῆς ("as the sand of the earth") — substituting "sand" (ἄμμος, ammos) for "dust" (עָפָר, afar). This is an interpretive variation: dust and sand share the quality of uncountable abundance, but dust (the fine powder of dry earth) is more elemental and more connected to the Adam-creation imagery of Genesis 2–3. The MT's dust is followed in the formal translation.

**13. אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא — "The Oaks of Mamre" (v. 18)**

The chapter closes with Abram settling "by the oaks of Mamre, which are at Hebron" (אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא אֲשֶׁר בְּחֶבְרוֹן):

וַיֵּשֶׁב אַבְרָם בְּאֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא אֲשֶׁר בְּחֶבְרוֹן
וַיִּבֶן שָׁם מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה

"So Abram moved his tent and came and settled by the oaks of Mamre, which are at Hebron, and he built there an altar to the LORD."

The word אֵלֹן (elon) is the same word used for the sacred tree site of Genesis 12:6 (אֵלוֹן מוֹרֶה, "the oak of Moreh"). Here the plural אֵלֹנֵי (eloné, "oaks of") designates a grove — the oaks of Mamre. The name Mamre will recur throughout the Abrahamic narrative (Genesis 14:13, 18:1, 23:17, 19) as the site of the divine visitation of Genesis 18 and as a landmark near Hebron. Mamre is introduced here without explanation — the text assumes or introduces a local name.

The word חֶבְרוֹן (Hebron) designates one of the most ancient cities of Canaan, located in the hill country south of Jerusalem at approximately 930 meters elevation. It will become the burial place of Abraham and Sarah (Genesis 23), of Isaac and Rebekah, of Jacob and Leah (Genesis 49:29–32). The choice of Hebron as Abram's settlement point is thus not merely geographical — it is the beginning of the patriarchal connection to a land that will become sacred through burial and memory.

The chapter ends as it has ended at every sacred stopping point since Genesis 12:7: Abram builds an altar to the LORD. This is the pattern of the patriarch: wherever he settles, wherever the LORD appears or promises, he builds an altar. The altars are acts of worship, acts of territorial claim in faith, and acts of naming — marking the land with the name of the LORD before he possesses a single foot of it as his own.

The LXX renders: τὴν δρῦν τὴν Μαμβρη — "the oak of Mambre." The LXX uses the singular (δρῦν, "oak") rather than the plural (the MT's אֵלֹנֵי, "oaks of"), suggesting either a different manuscript tradition or a harmonizing of the site description to the singular tree motif of 12:6.

**14. The Connective Tissue of Verses 5–9 — Abram's Generosity**

The generosity Abram displays in verses 8–9 is remarkable in its context. Abram is the elder, the patriarch, the one to whom the divine promise was made. Lot is a dependent nephew traveling under Abram's patronage (Genesis 12:4: "Lot went with him"). By the social conventions of the ancient Near East, Abram had every right to choose first. Instead, he offers Lot the entire choice:

"Is not the whole land before you? Separate yourself from me. If you take the left hand, then I will go to the right, or if you take the right hand, then I will go to the left."

The rhetorical question — "Is not the whole land before you?" (הֲלֹא כָל הָאָרֶץ לְפָנֶיךָ, halo' kol ha'arets lefanekha) — opens the choice to Lot without reservation. The directional alternatives ("left" and "right," שְׂמֹאל and יָמִין) cover every possibility. Abram accepts whatever is left over. This is an act of extraordinary magnanimity — the more so because it is Abram's land by divine promise, not Lot's.

The formal translation preserves the conditional structure ("if you take the left hand... if you take the right hand") that renders the Hebrew's exact parallel: אִם הַשְּׂמֹאל... וְאִם הַיָּמִין. The LXX agrees closely: εἰ σὺ εἰς ἀριστερά, ἐγὼ εἰς δεξιά· εἰ δὲ σὺ εἰς δεξιά, ἐγὼ εἰς ἀριστερά — "if you to the left, I to the right; if you to the right, I to the left."

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 13

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 8–9 (GEN_013_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_013_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The chapter heading "CAP. XIII" is visible at the top of page 8. The following variants from the MT are noted:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 13:1 | וַיַּעַל ("went up") | Ἀνέβη ("went up") | "went up" | Full agreement; directional verb preserved in both |
| 13:3 | וַיִּסַּע לְמַסָּעָיו ("journeyed by his stages") | ἐπορεύθη ὅθεν ἦλθεν ("went from where he had come") | "journeyed on" | LXX collapses the itinerary formula; MT's stage-movement is more precise |
| 13:7 | הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי ("the Canaanite and the Perizzite") | ὁ Χαναναῖος καὶ ὁ Φερεζαῖος (same) | "the Canaanite and the Perizzite" | Full agreement in substance |
| 13:10 | כְּגַן יְהוָה ("like the garden of the LORD") | ὡς ὁ παράδεισος τοῦ θεοῦ ("like the paradise of God") | "like the garden of the LORD" | LXX uses παράδεισος ("paradise") for גַּן; MT's "garden" is more intimate and Edenic; MT followed |
| 13:10 | שַׁחֵת יְהוָה ("the LORD destroyed") | καταστρέψαι τὸν θεόν ("God overthrew") | "the LORD destroyed" | LXX uses "God" (θεόν) for YHWH; follows standard LXX practice; MT's YHWH preserved |
| 13:13 | לַיהוָה מְאֹד ("against the LORD exceedingly") | ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ σφόδρα ("before God exceedingly") | "against the LORD" | LXX's ἐναντίον ("before") softens the adversarial preposition of MT's לַ; MT retained |
| 13:16 | כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ ("as the dust of the earth") | ὡς τὴν ἄμμον τῆς γῆς ("as the sand of the earth") | "as the dust of the earth" | LXX substitutes sand for dust; MT's dust has stronger connection to Gen. 2–3 Adam imagery; MT followed |
| 13:18 | אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא ("oaks of Mamre") | τὴν δρῦν τὴν Μαμβρη ("the oak of Mambre") | "the oaks of Mamre" | LXX uses singular "oak"; MT's plural "oaks" / grove reading followed |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 13 — Two Responses to the Same Land**

Genesis 13 is constructed around a single geographic and theological contrast: two men stand in the same land, and one chooses by sight while the other receives by promise. The structure of the chapter enacts this contrast in three movements:

**Movement 1 — Return and Conflict (vv. 1–7):** Abram returns from Egypt enriched, retracing his journey to Bethel where he first worshiped. The blessing of material abundance (v. 2) generates conflict (vv. 6–7). The problem is created by excess, not deficiency.

**Movement 2 — Separation and Choice (vv. 8–13):** Abram proposes separation with magnanimous generosity. Lot chooses the Jordan plain by its visual appeal — it is well-watered, fertile, Eden-like. Lot moves east (v. 11) — a directional echo in Genesis of movement away from blessing: Adam and Eve are expelled "east of Eden" (Genesis 3:24); Cain goes to dwell "east of Eden" (Genesis 4:16); the builders of Babel journey "from the east" (Genesis 11:2). Lot's eastward move positions him with those who have moved away from the garden. He settles by Sodom, the most sinful city in the land.

**Movement 3 — Promise and Worship (vv. 14–18):** After Lot departs, the LORD speaks to Abram for the first time since Genesis 12:7. God gives Abram the comprehensive promise of the land and of innumerable offspring. Abram receives what Lot chose by sight — and receives it in a form that transcends what the eye can see. Lot looked and chose; God commands Abram to look and then promises. Abram builds an altar.

**The East-West / Sight-Faith Axis**

The directional movement of the chapter encodes its theology:
- Lot lifts his eyes and looks (v. 10) — self-directed, motivated by appearance
- Lot chooses and moves east (v. 11) — away from the center of promise
- Lot settles by Sodom (v. 12) — the destination of self-chosen abundance
- God commands Abram to look north, south, east, and west (v. 14) — comprehensive, commanded vision
- God gives Abram the land in every direction he sees (v. 15) — promise replaces sight
- Abram settles at Hebron and builds an altar (v. 18) — worship as the response to promise

The narrative does not depict Lot as villainous or Abram as perfect. Lot chooses rationally, as any prudent herdsman would, based on the best available land. The text does not condemn him explicitly. But it positions his choice — the choice that looks like wisdom — immediately before the announcement that his chosen city is "exceedingly wicked and sinful against the LORD" (v. 13). Sight, however accurate, does not see what is morally and eschatologically true.

**The Land Promise — Forever (עַד עוֹלָם)**

Verse 15 contains the word עוֹלָם (olam), rendered "forever": "all the land that you see I will give to you and to your offspring forever." This is the first use of the temporal term עוֹלָם in connection with the land promise. The word עוֹלָם designates an indefinitely long duration — "long time, eternity, forever" — without specifying a precise upper limit. It functions as the strongest available Hebrew temporal intensifier: the land promise is not temporary or conditional on any circumstance mentioned here. The formal translation renders "forever" — the standard English equivalent.

**The Altar Pattern**

Genesis 13 records two altar connections: the return to the altar between Bethel and Ai (v. 4, the altar first built in Genesis 12:8) and the building of a new altar at the oaks of Mamre near Hebron (v. 18). The altar at Bethel represents Abram's return to the beginning — a resumption of the journey of faith after the Egypt detour. The altar at Hebron represents a new beginning: a new settlement, a new promise, a new act of worship in response to the renewed divine address. Wherever Abram goes in Canaan, he builds altars. The land is being marked with worship long before it is possessed. This is the pattern of faith the chapter commends.
